introduction: A common neurodegenerative disease that results from deficiency of the neurotransmitter dopamine, in the striato-nigral pathway. The idiopathic variety is called Parkinson's disease while parkinsonism is used for secondary cases.

features: Rest tremors: coarse, distal tremors described as pill-rolling type
Loss of arm swinging when walking
Gait changes: shuffling gait with flexed posturing
Multi-system atrophy - Parkinsonism with postural hypotension (Shy-Dragger Syndrome)
With brain stem and cerebellar degeneration (Olivo-ponto-cerebellar syndrome)
With Dementia – Dementia with Lewy Body characterized by fluctuating confusion, visual hallucination and phenothiazine sensitivity.

investigations: Neuro-imaging: CT scan/MRI for exclusion of possible differentials

goals: Ensure mobility and avoidance of falls
drug: L-dopa/carbidopa (dose expressed as levodopa): 50 mg orally every 6 - 8 hours increased by 100 mg once or twice weekly depending on response
Trihexyphenidyl (benzhexol) 1 mg orally daily, increased gradually (usually 5 - 15 mg in 3 - 4 divided doses up to a maximum of 20 mg)

Bromocriptine : First week: 1 - 1.25 mg orally nocte, Second week: 2 - 2.25 mg nocte, Third week: 2.5 mg twice daily, Fourth week: 2.5 mg three times daily; increasing by 2.5 mg every 1 - 2 weeks according to response (usual range is 10 - 40 mg daily)
Ropinirole: 1 - 3 mg orally once daily (in resistant cases)
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Dopamine replacement drugs: dyskinesia, neuropathic pain
Advisable to start with small doses and gradually increase
Need for dosage and timing adjustments when side effects manifest
Dopa-agonists: postural hypotension; may cause vomiting
Caution is advised to avoid falls
Anticholinergic drugs: constipation; memory problems and double vision.
Their use is contraindicated in the presence of glaucoma and prostatic enlargement
supportive_measures: Antidepressants such as amitriptyline for pain (which could be quite incapacitating) especially with dopamine-replacement drugs

complications: Recurrent falls with attendant complications e.g. fractures, subdural haematoma
prevention: Avoid identified causative agents where feasible
Timely and appropriate treatment to prevent/reduce complications
